item.title: Orion Belt vs Teleport — a practical Teleport alternative for SSH PAM
item.description: Compare Orion Belt and Teleport for self-hosted privileged access. When a lighter SSH bastion/PAM gateway wins, when Teleport’s broader platform is worth it, plus Boundary and classic bastions.
item.content:encoded:
Short version: Orion Belt is a self-hosted SSH access gateway with PAM workflows. Teleport is a broad zero-trust access platform. If you mainly need mediated SSH, recording, JIT, and MFA — without inbound ports on every host or a large ops surface — Orion Belt is the lighter Teleport alternative.

This comparison is for practitioners evaluating open source PAM / self-hosted privileged access options for SSH, not a dunk contest. Licensing differs; verify current Teleport terms for your use case. Orion Belt is Apache 2.0 + Commons Clause (details).

Who should read this​

  • Teams searching for a Teleport alternative focused on SSH
  • Homelab and SMB operators who find Teleport’s surface area large
  • Security engineers comparing bastion vs platform approaches

Side-by-side​

DimensionOrion BeltTeleportHashiCorp BoundaryTraditional bastion
Primary jobSSH PAM / bastion gatewayMulti-protocol zero-trust accessIdentity-aware session brokeringNetwork jump point
Protocols (typical)SSH / SCP (focused)SSH, K8s, DB, web, Windows, …Many via targets/workersWhatever you tunnel
How targets are reachedAgents dial out (reverse SSH)Node agents / tunnels / IoTWorkers / proxiesInbound to bastion; often inbound to hosts
Inbound on targetsNot required for agent pathDepends on modeDepends on layoutCommon
Session recordingYes (+ live watch)YesYes (with recording path)DIY
JIT / approvalsBuilt-in (+ ChatOps)Access requests / rolesVia IdP / workflowsRare
MFATOTP + WebAuthn; FIDO SSH keysStrong IdP / MFA storyVia IdPDIY
AuthorizationReBAC (+ optional OpenFGA)RBAC / roles / traitsIAM-centricOS users / keys
SSH CAOptional built-inFirst-class cert storyDifferent modelManual / none
Ops weightSmaller SSH-focused deployLarger platformMedium–largeSmall but feature-poor
HostingSelf-hostedSelf-hosted or cloudSelf-hosted or HCPSelf-hosted

Architecture contrast​

Traditional bastion​

Operator → (SSH) → Bastion → (SSH) → Target

You still manage keys, often lack recording/JIT, and frequently still expose SSH somewhere on the path.

Orion Belt​

Operator → Gateway ← (reverse SSH) ← Agent on target

The target initiates the tunnel. The gateway brokers sessions, enforces policy, and can record or live-watch. Background: Why reverse SSH agents.

Teleport-shaped platforms​

Powerful and broad: many protocols, deep identity integration, large feature surface — and correspondingly more to deploy, learn, and operate.

When to choose Orion Belt​

Choose Orion Belt if most of these are true:

  • Privileged access is mostly SSH
  • You want no inbound SSH on application hosts
  • You want recording + live watch + JIT without a PAM appliance
  • You prefer self-hosted, Linux-first, inspectable code
  • You accept a narrower scope today (OIDC, HA, other protocols on the roadmap)

Hands-on: Try in 10 minutes.

When Teleport (or similar) is a better fit​

Choose Teleport if you need:

  • First-class Kubernetes / DB / Windows / web access in one product now
  • A large ecosystem and org-wide zero-trust program already centered on that tool
  • Features Orion Belt has not shipped yet

When Boundary fits better​

Boundary shines when the center of gravity is identity + credential brokering in a HashiCorp world — not “SSH bastion with PAM workflows” as the primary UX.

Security tradeoffs (reverse agents)​

Gain: targets need outbound reachability to the gateway, not inbound SSH from attackers’ scan range.
Cost: offline agents are unreachable; the gateway is a high-value control plane — harden it, encrypt recordings, require MFA for admins.

SEO-style FAQ​

Is Orion Belt a Teleport alternative?
For SSH-centric self-hosted PAM/bastion needs, yes. For full multi-protocol zero trust, Teleport remains broader.

Is Orion Belt open source?
Source-available under Apache 2.0 + Commons Clause — free to self-host; not for sale as a product/SaaS wrapper. Not OSI “Open Source” in the strict sense.

Can I migrate from a jump host?
Yes: enroll agents, move humans to user+host@gateway, turn on recording, then tighten inbound SSH. See bastion vs PAM.

Try before you debate​

git clone https://github.com/orion-belt-dev/orion-belt.git
cd orion-belt
./scripts/docker-quickstart.sh

Documentation · GitHub · Discord

item.title: Orion Belt v1.0 — self-hosted SSH PAM gateway is stable
item.description: Orion Belt v1.0 is released — self-hosted SSH access gateway with reverse agents, session recording, JIT, MFA, and ReBAC. Try the 10-minute path and join early operators.
item.content:encoded:
Orion Belt v1.0 is out: a stable, self-hosted SSH access gateway with PAM workflows. Reverse agents, session recording, live watch, JIT approvals, MFA/WebAuthn, ReBAC, optional SSH CA, Linux packages, and a web console — without a SaaS dependency.

This is the release note for operators who have been waiting for a version line we are willing to call production-shaped for the SSH slice.

What “v1.0” means here​

  • SSH proxy / bastion path we will not casually break
  • Documented Docker quickstart and package installs
  • Recording + live watch on the gateway path
  • JIT + ChatOps approvals
  • MFA options for the console
  • ReBAC (optional OpenFGA)
  • Optional SSH CA
  • OpenAPI + observability docs in the main repo

Still on the roadmap (not pretend-done): OIDC, HA, additional protocols. Honesty > checkbox theater — lessons.

Positioning​

Teleport-like SSH access control — without opening inbound ports on every host or adopting a large platform.

Compare: Orion Belt vs Teleport. Landscape: open-source PAM guide.

Try it in about ten minutes​

git clone https://github.com/orion-belt-dev/orion-belt.git
cd orion-belt
./scripts/docker-quickstart.sh

Then: Try Orion Belt in 10 minutes through agent → SSH → Sessions playback.

License​

Apache 2.0 + Commons Clause — free to self-host and use internally; you cannot sell Orion Belt as a product or substantially derived hosted service. Plain-language FAQ.

Join early operators​

We want deployments and sharp feedback more than vanity metrics. Early operators call · Discord · Discussions

Links​

  • orion-belt.dev
  • GitHub
  • Documentation

item.title: Join Orion Belt early operators — first self-hosted SSH PAM deployments
item.description: We are looking for the first ~20 teams and labs running Orion Belt v1.0. Get install help, influence the roadmap, and help shape a self-hosted Teleport alternative for SSH.
item.content:encoded:
GitHub stars are a lagging indicator. The leading indicator we care about is simple: are serious people deploying Orion Belt and telling us what breaks?

This is an open call for early operators — roughly the first twenty labs or small teams running v1.0 as a real self-hosted SSH PAM / bastion path.

Who we want​

  • Homelab / self-hosting users with more than one Linux box
  • Small company SRE/DevOps teams needing recording + JIT without SaaS
  • Security engineers evaluating a Teleport alternative for SSH-only scope
  • People willing to file reproducible bugs (Docker quickstart welcome)

Who we are not optimizing for (yet)​

  • Enterprises needing HA + OIDC + multi-protocol on day one
  • Teams blocked by Commons Clause / non-OSI policy (license notes)
  • Drive-by “please add my niche protocol” without deploying SSH first

What you get​

  • Direct help on Discord when agents will not dial out
  • Faster eyes on your issues and PRs
  • Input on roadmap sequencing (OIDC, HA, etc.)
  • Optional public credit as an early operator

What we ask​

  1. Complete Try in 10 minutes
  2. Leave it running on something you actually use (even a homelab VM)
  3. Open GitHub issues with versions, logs, and repro steps
  4. Tell us if docs lied

How to raise your hand​

  • Message in Discord
  • Open a GitHub Discussion
  • Or comment on an issue that you are running v1.0 in anger

Metrics we track privately for the first month: install attempts, active deployments, external issues — not vanity follower counts. Background: lessons building PAM.

Related​

  • v1.0 announcement
  • Why reverse SSH agents

item.title: Lessons learned building a self-hosted SSH PAM gateway
item.description: Practical lessons from building Orion Belt — access control vs audit, UX for JIT, reverse tunnel tradeoffs, licensing honesty, and keeping a 10-minute install path sacred.
item.content:encoded:
Orion Belt v1.0 is our line in the sand for a stable self-hosted SSH PAM slice. These are the lessons that shaped it — useful if you are building similar infrastructure or evaluating whether our tradeoffs match yours.

1. Access control is easy compared to audit​

ReBAC graphs are approachable. Faithful, replayable, retainable session recordings are not. Budget engineering time accordingly. Deep dive: Session recording is hard.

2. UX beats feature count for JIT​

If temporary access takes longer than sharing a key, standing keys win. ChatOps approvals and a short CLI/UI path matter more than a twelfth policy DSL. See JIT access.

3. Reverse tunnels move risk; they don’t delete it​

Agents dialing out remove inbound SSH on targets and concentrate trust on the gateway. Harden the gateway like a directory service. Explained in Why reverse SSH agents.

4. Optional complexity must stay optional​

SSH CA, OpenFGA, and WebAuthn are valuable. None of them should block the first recorded session. Optional flags exist so labs stay fast (SSH CA, MFA).

5. License clarity is a security feature​

People evaluating PAM software are professionally paranoid. Commons Clause disclosed early builds trust even when it loses some adopters. License FAQ.

6. Protect the 10-minute path like an API​

Every feature PR should ask: did we break docker-quickstart → agent → Sessions? If yes, fix or document before merge. Install path design.

7. Say no to protocol sprawl in v1​

Kubernetes and database proxies are real needs. Shipping a mediocre version of each would dilute the SSH promise. Roadmap honesty > checkbox theater. Comparison mindset: vs Teleport.

8. Dual packaging is worth it​

Docker converts; packages retain. Packages and labs.

What we want next from the community​

Sharp issues from people who deployed something — not drive-by feature wishes. If that is you: early operators.

Related​

  • Announcing Orion Belt
  • Discord · GitHub

item.title: Shipping deb/rpm/apk packages and a Docker lab for SSH PAM
item.description: Why Orion Belt ships native Linux packages and a Docker quickstart — two audiences for self-hosted SSH bastion installs, signed repos, and reproducible bug reports.
item.content:encoded:
Real gateways want systemd and signed package repos. First-time evaluators want docker compose and a browser. Orion Belt ships both on purpose. This post explains the split for operators installing a self-hosted SSH bastion / PAM stack.

Two audiences, two artifacts​

AudienceSuccess looks likeArtifact
Security / platform engSurvives reboot, upgrades, SELinuxdeb / rpm / apk + docs
Curious SRE on a laptopRecorded session in 10 minutesdocker-quickstart.sh

If you only ship packages, blog traffic bounces. If you only ship Docker, production teams bounce. Document them as siblings, not one confused path — see 10-minute install path.

What “make packages” is for​

From the product repo:

make packages
# optional signed repos with ORION_REQUIRE_SIGN=1

You get installable server, agent, and tools packages for common Linux distros. First-run after packages: server YAML, setup, Add agent — covered in Server setup and the main repo SETUP.md / PACKAGING.md.

What Docker is for​

  • Marketing conversion
  • Reproducing GitHub issues
  • Homelabs and CI smoke tests
  • Demoing reverse agents without cloud VMs

When someone says “agent won’t connect,” asking for a Docker quickstart repro saves days of “what’s in your custom unit file?”

Signed repos and supply chain​

GPG-signed APT/RPM trees matter once you leave the lab. Treat packaging keys like release keys: generate, backup, publish the public key, and document fingerprint verification. The scripts in-tree (gen-packaging-key.sh, publish-repos.sh) exist so you are not inventing that ceremony under pressure.

FAQ​

Can I run production on Docker Compose?
You can. Many teams still prefer packages + systemd for host networking and upgrade habits. Compose is not immoral; just own backup/restore for volumes.

Do agents need the same package format as the server?
Match the target OS. The UI install script path is built around that.

Related​

  • Try in 10 minutes
  • Lessons building PAM
  • GitHub

item.title: SSH bastion host vs PAM gateway — which problem are you solving?
item.description: Clarify bastion hosts versus privileged access management (PAM) for SSH. When a jump box is enough, when you need recording and JIT, and where Orion Belt fits.
item.content:encoded:
Engineers say “bastion” to mean three different products. That ambiguity wastes evaluation time. This article separates SSH bastion hosts from PAM gateways, and places Orion Belt on that map.

Definitions that help​

Jump host / classic bastion​

A hardened server you SSH into, then pivot to internal hosts. Network rendezvous. Policy and audit are usually DIY.

SSH access gateway / recording bastion​

A control plane that brokers SSH, often with session recording and centralized authn. May use reverse tunnels or agents.

Privileged access management (PAM)​

A broader discipline: joiner-mover-leaver, credential vaults, password rotation, multi-protocol privileged sessions, tickets, and compliance reports. SSH is one slice.

Decision table​

NeedJump hostSSH gatewayFull PAM suite
Reach internal SSH✓✓✓
Per-machine grants✗ / DIY✓✓
Session recording✗ / DIY✓✓
JIT approvals✗✓ / varies✓
K8s/DB/Windows too✗✗ / limited✓
Lowest ops cost✓MediumHigh

Where Orion Belt fits​

Orion Belt is a self-hosted SSH PAM gateway (bastion + PAM workflows for SSH):

  • Reverse agents (dial out)
  • Recording + live watch
  • JIT + MFA + ReBAC
  • Not a full multi-protocol PAM suite in v1

If your RFP says “PAM” but the checklist is SSH + audit + approvals, a focused gateway may close the RFP without a platform migration. If the checklist includes password checkout for Windows and database proxying next quarter, shortlist broader tools — see open-source PAM landscape and vs Teleport.

Migration pattern we see​

  1. Today: jump host + VPN
  2. Pain: audits, shared keys, inbound SSH
  3. Next: SSH gateway with recording (Orion Belt class)
  4. Later: expand protocols or IdP integration as needed (OIDC on our roadmap)

Do not buy step 4 on day one unless step 4’s problems are already on fire.

Related​

  • Why reverse SSH agents
  • Announcing Orion Belt

item.title: Live SSH session watch for self-hosted PAM gateways
item.description: How live session watch works on an SSH bastion, privacy and permission concerns, and how Orion Belt lets admins observe active privileged sessions safely.
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes playback tomorrow is too late. Live session watch lets an authorized admin observe an active SSH (or web terminal) session as it happens — useful for incident response, mentoring, and verifying break-glass work.

Used badly, it becomes silent surveillance. Used well, it is a sharp tool in a self-hosted PAM gateway. Here is how we think about it in Orion Belt.

Recording vs live watch​

Session recordingLive watch
TimingPersisted cast for laterStreaming view of an active session
Primary useCompliance, forensicsReal-time oversight
Failure modeDisk secrets / retentionPrivacy / overreach

Both sit on the gateway path. Direct-to-host SSH remains invisible. Cultural rule still applies: session recording is hard.

Product requirements we care about​

  1. Explicit action — an admin opens watch; it is not ambient for every operator
  2. Authorization — not every user role can watch every session
  3. Honest lifecycle — when the session ends, watch ends
  4. Same trust boundary as recording — if you can watch, you are already in the sensitive-data club

When to use it​

  • On-call verifying a risky production change
  • Approver stays online during a JIT window (JIT access)
  • Training: senior watches junior on a staging host
  • Suspected account misuse (with clear policy)

When not to use it​

  • Routine browsing of everyone’s terminals “because we can”
  • Legal environments without disclosed monitoring policy
  • As a substitute for proper pairing tools when the goal is collaboration, not control

Operator tips​

  • Prefer watching JIT sessions you already approved
  • Prefer staging for training watches
  • Assume anything typed can be seen — same as recording
  • Combine with ChatOps approvals so the human story is in one timeline

Try it after a lab SSH in Try in 10 minutes — open Sessions while the session is still active.

Related​

  • Sessions guide
  • Security FAQ

item.title: Apache 2.0 + Commons Clause — what Orion Belt’s license means
item.description: Clear explanation of Orion Belt’s Apache 2.0 + Commons Clause license for self-hosted PAM — what you can do, what you cannot sell, and why we disclose it on HN and Reddit.
item.content:encoded:
Security infrastructure requires trust. Burying a restrictive clause until after launch is how open-source communities learn to ignore you. Orion Belt uses Apache License 2.0 with the Commons Clause, and we say so in the README, website, and launch posts.

This article is the plain-language license FAQ for people evaluating Orion Belt as self-hosted privileged access software.

What you can do​

Under Apache 2.0 + Commons Clause (as we ship it):

  • Use Orion Belt internally, including for commercial companies
  • Modify the code
  • Self-host for your team / customers’ infrastructure you operate as part of your own business (you are not selling Orion as the product)
  • Read and audit the source

What you cannot do​

The Commons Clause withholds the right to Sell the software. In practice for Orion Belt:

  • You may not sell Orion Belt as a product
  • You may not offer a hosted service whose value derives substantially from Orion Belt as the product you are selling

If you want to build a managed Orion SaaS as your business, that is exactly what the clause is designed to stop without permission.

Is it “Open Source”?​

OSI “Open Source” has a specific meaning. Commons Clause software is often called source-available. We do not pretend otherwise on Hacker News, Lobsters, or Reddit.

If your organization requires OSI-approved licenses only, Orion Belt may be disallowed — better to know before you invest evaluation time.

Why not AGPL or a pure Apache 2.0?​

Different projects pick different weapons. Our goals:

  1. Keep the code auditable for security buyers
  2. Allow free self-hosting for real users
  3. Prevent a cloud vendor from wrapping the repo as their PAM SaaS without a deal

Whether you agree with that tradeoff is personal. We would rather be declined honestly than adopted under false premises.

How we talk about it in public​

Launch checklist for maintainers:

  • One sentence in Show HN / Reddit
  • Link to LICENSE
  • No “fully open source” claims without qualification

Full legal text: LICENSE · commonsclause.com

Related​

  • Announcing Orion Belt
  • Open-source PAM landscape

item.title: Optional SSH certificate authority for self-hosted bastions
item.description: When to enable an SSH CA in a PAM gateway, how short-lived certificates reduce key sprawl, and why Orion Belt keeps SSH CA optional for labs.
item.content:encoded:
Long-lived authorized_keys entries are how SSH access rots. An SSH certificate authority (CA) issues short-lived user and host certificates instead. Powerful — and easy to force on users before they have a working bastion.

Orion Belt treats SSH CA as optional. You can run reverse agents, recording, JIT, and MFA without it. This article explains when to turn CA on for a self-hosted SSH PAM deployment.

What an SSH CA changes​

Without CAWith CA
Standing user keys on many systemsShort-lived user certs from the gateway
TOFU / known_hosts driftHost certificates for gateway/agents
Revocation by hunting filesWait for expiry (+ revoke CA-side as designed)

OpenSSH has supported certificates for years; PAM/bastion products wrap the operational bits humans forget.

Why optional?​

Because the 10-minute path should not require understanding certificate principals, AuthorizedPrincipalsFile, and CA key ceremonies. Labs need wow-first. CA is a production maturity step — see Designing a 10-minute install path.

When you should enable it​

Enable SSH CA when:

  • Key inventory is already painful
  • Contractors come and go weekly
  • You want host identity pinned via Host CA
  • Someone on the team can own CA key backup / rotation

Keep it off when:

  • You are still validating reverse agents and recording
  • Nobody owns secret management for the CA master key

Orion encrypts CA material at rest when enabled (ssh_ca.master_key style config in the main repo). Do not use example secrets.

Operational checklist​

  1. Backup the CA master key like a root of trust
  2. Set host principals to the DNS/IPs clients actually use
  3. Document how agents receive host certs on enrollment
  4. Monitor expiry and clock skew (certs are time-sensitive)
  5. Pair with recording — certificates are not an audit trail

Full reference: SSH_CA.md in the product repo.

Related​

  • MFA and WebAuthn
  • ReBAC for SSH machines
  • Security best practices

item.title: MFA and WebAuthn for a self-hosted SSH PAM console
item.description: How Orion Belt splits console MFA (TOTP, WebAuthn) from SSH public keys and FIDO sk- keys — configuration pitfalls, rp_id origins, and practical hardening.
item.content:encoded:
Operators will reject MFA that fights SSH muscle memory. Orion Belt splits the problem: protect the web console with TOTP and WebAuthn, and keep SSH on keys — including FIDO sk-* keys — that OpenSSH already understands.

If you are hardening a self-hosted privileged access gateway, this is the MFA model to expect and the footguns to avoid.

Two planes of authentication​

PlaneMechanismsGoal
Web console /uiSSH pubkey login, password+TOTP, WebAuthnProtect admin/approver UX
SSH to gatewayRegistered user keys, optional FIDO SSH keysDay-to-day access

Confusing these planes creates tickets like “my YubiKey works in the browser but not in SSH” — different protocols, different registration paths.

Console MFA​

TOTP​

Familiar authenticator-app second factor. Useful when hardware keys are unavailable.

WebAuthn / FIDO2​

Browser security keys bound to:

  • rp_id — hostname only (e.g. orion.example.com or localhost)
  • origins — full origins the UI is served from (https://orion.example.com, maybe a Vite dev origin)

If rp_id / origins disagree with the URL in the address bar, registration and login fail in opaque ways. Fix config before blaming the YubiKey.

Docker env knobs include ORION_WEBAUTHN_ENABLED, ORION_PUBLIC_HOST, and ORION_PUBLIC_ORIGIN — see .env.server.example in the repo.

SSH keys and FIDO​

Users register SSH public keys on their Orion identity. OpenSSH then connects as:

ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -p 2222 alice+web-01@gateway

For hardware-backed SSH keys (sk-ssh-ed25519@openssh.com, etc.), register the public key the same way and touch the device when OpenSSH prompts. Details: main repo docs/openssh-clients.md.

Recommended hardening ladder​

  1. Lab: pubkey admin via quickstart
  2. Staging: enable TOTP for admins
  3. Production: WebAuthn for admins/approvers + network allowlist on UI
  4. Require MFA for sensitive console actions as your threat model demands

Also encrypt session recordings — MFA does not help if casts sit plaintext on disk (session recording).

FAQ​

Can I force MFA on every SSH connection?
SSH MFA UX varies; many teams prefer strong keys + gateway policy + recorded sessions. Console MFA is the high-ROI control for approvals and replay.

Does WebAuthn replace SSH keys?
No. It protects browser sessions to the control plane.

Related​

  • Security guide
  • Optional SSH CA
  • Try in 10 minutes

item.title: How we designed a 10-minute SSH PAM install path
item.description: Why Orion Belt optimizes for docker-quickstart → agent dial-out → recorded SSH. Building a conversion-friendly self-hosted bastion onboarding experience.
item.content:encoded:
Most infrastructure products document everything. Conversion dies on page one. For Orion Belt, the path we care about is short on purpose:

gateway up → agent dials out → SSH works → session recorded → wow

This post explains why that sequence matters for a self-hosted SSH bastion / PAM product, what we automated in docker-quickstart.sh, and what we deliberately left for production docs.

The funnel that actually matters​

Stars and HN traffic are useless if the README dumps people into YAML archaeology. Security tools especially need a trusted first success: if the demo lies, nobody will harden a real deployment.

We optimize for:

  1. Clone the repo
  2. One script starts Postgres + gateway + admin bootstrap
  3. Browser login with an SSH public key
  4. Add agent → outbound connect
  5. Open a session → see it under Sessions

Guide: Try Orion Belt in 10 minutes.

What the quickstart script does​

./scripts/docker-quickstart.sh:

  • Writes .env.server with random Postgres password + JWT secret if missing
  • docker compose up for the server stack
  • Waits for /health
  • Generates an admin ed25519 keypair if needed
  • Runs setup to create the first admin

There is no self-service signup form. That is intentional: the control plane is SSH-key identity, not a random password form on the internet.

Why Docker for labs and packages for prod​

AudiencePath
Evaluator / bug reproDocker quickstart + compose agent
Homelab lasting a weekendDocker or packages
Company gatewaydeb/rpm/apk + hardening docs

Both are real. Collapsing them into one doc produces a bad lab and a bad production guide. See Linux packages and labs.

Friction we refuse to ship in the first ten minutes​

  • Manual SQL
  • Hand-editing JWT secrets without a generator
  • Requiring a public DNS name before hello-world
  • Forcing SSH CA on

Those belong after the wow.

Measure the path​

If you fork or contribute, break the 10-minute path on purpose and see what fails. Every failure mode should either be fixed or documented in the troubleshooting table — not discovered on HN launch day.

Related​

  • Why reverse SSH agents
  • Server setup
  • GitHub

item.title: Open-source PAM and SSH bastion options compared
item.description: A practical guide to open-source privileged access and SSH bastions — Teleport, Boundary, Warpgate, DIY jump hosts, and Orion Belt as a self-hosted SSH-focused PAM gateway.
item.content:encoded:
Search for open source PAM or Teleport alternative and you will find overlapping tools that solve different jobs. This guide maps the landscape for SSH-first teams: what each category is good at, where it gets heavy, and where Orion Belt fits as a self-hosted SSH bastion / PAM gateway.

Categories that get conflated​

1. Jump hosts / classic bastions​

A hardened box you SSH into, then hop onward. Cheap to start. Weak on recording, JIT, and per-machine policy unless you invent them.

2. SSH gateways / recording proxies​

Tools that terminate or broker SSH and may record sessions (Warpgate-like products, custom sshd ForceCommand setups, etc.). Stronger audit story; feature depth varies.

3. Identity-centric brokers​

HashiCorp Boundary emphasizes identity and credential brokering in a Hashi ecosystem. Excellent if that is already your center of gravity; different UX if you mainly wanted an SSH bastion.

4. Broad zero-trust platforms​

Teleport (and similar) cover SSH plus Kubernetes, databases, web apps, Windows, and a large policy surface. Ideal when you need that breadth; heavy when you only needed SSH PAM.

5. Commercial PAM appliances​

CyberArk / BeyondTrust class: deep enterprise workflows, price and complexity to match.

Comparison table​

OptionBest atWatch-outs
DIY jump hostTiny labsNo JIT/recording by default
Warpgate-style gatewaySimple shared SSH gatewayPAM workflows may be thinner
BoundaryIdentity + Hashi stackNot “SSH bastion UX” first
TeleportMulti-protocol zero trustOps and learning curve
Orion BeltSelf-hosted SSH PAM slice, reverse agentsNarrower protocol scope in v1
Commercial PAMEnterprise process depthCost, lock-in

Where Orion Belt sits​

Orion Belt optimizes for:

  • Self-hosted privileged access focused on SSH/SCP
  • Agents dial out (no inbound SSH required on targets)
  • Session recording + live watch
  • JIT approvals (+ ChatOps)
  • MFA / WebAuthn, ReBAC, optional SSH CA
  • Linux packages + Docker quickstart

It is intentionally a lighter Teleport alternative for teams whose privileged access problem is mostly SSH — not a claim to replace every Teleport protocol.

Deep dive: Orion Belt vs Teleport. Architecture: Why reverse SSH agents.

How to choose in one meeting​

Ask:

  1. Do we need non-SSH protocols this quarter?
  2. Must targets stay free of inbound SSH?
  3. Is session recording a hard requirement?
  4. Do we need JIT approvals or is standing access OK?
  5. Can we run a gateway (Postgres, backups) ourselves?
  6. Does license policy allow Apache 2.0 + Commons Clause?

If answers skew toward SSH + self-host + recording/JIT, evaluate Orion Belt hands-on:

Try in 10 minutes

Related reading​

  • Announcing Orion Belt
  • Bastion vs PAM
  • Commons Clause honesty

item.title: Just-in-time (JIT) SSH access with approvals that people will use
item.description: How to design JIT privileged access for SSH — request, approve, expire — with ChatOps and session recording. Orion Belt’s approach to temporary access workflows.
item.content:encoded:
Standing SSH privileges are convenient until an ex-contractor key still works on Friday night. Just-in-time (JIT) access — request, approve, expire — is the standard answer in privileged access management. It only works if the workflow is faster than pasting a key into Slack.

This article covers how JIT fits a self-hosted SSH PAM gateway, what UX details matter, and how Orion Belt implements temporary access without making on-call hate you.

Why standing access fails​

  • Keys outlive tickets
  • “Emergency” grants become permanent
  • Audit asks “who had root last month?” and the answer is “everyone in #ops”
  • Compliance frameworks increasingly expect time-bound privileged sessions

Commercial PAM vendors built empires on this pain. Open-source and self-hosted teams still under-serve it.

Anatomy of a usable JIT flow​

A flow people will follow:

  1. Engineer needs web-01 for 90 minutes
  2. They submit a reason + duration (CLI or UI)
  3. Approver gets a ping (console bell and/or ChatOps)
  4. Approval creates a grant that auto-expires
  5. Session is recorded while they work
  6. Grant disappears; recording remains for review

A flow people will bypass:

  1. Open four tabs of wiki
  2. File a ticket in a system nobody watches at 2am
  3. Wait for a manager who is asleep
  4. …so they reuse yesterday’s shared key

Orion Belt’s building blocks​

  • Access requests with reason and duration
  • Approvals in the web console
  • ChatOps plugins for Slack, Discord, Teams, Rocket.Chat — approvals where the team already lives
  • Recording + live watch on the gateway path
  • ReBAC grants underneath (see ReBAC for SSH machines)

Example CLI shape:

osh web-01 --request-access --duration 1h --reason "Incident 4821 rollback"

ChatOps without turning chat into your IdP​

Chat approvals are great for speed. They are not a substitute for:

  • Authenticating the approver properly
  • Logging who approved what
  • Ensuring the bot cannot be spoofed casually

Keep ChatOps as a notification + action surface bound back to the gateway’s authz, not a free-for-all emoji vote.

Pair JIT with recording​

Temporary access without audit just delays standing privilege. The conversion story we optimize for in docs is:

request → approve → SSH → Sessions playback

Lab walkthrough: Try in 10 minutes.

Policy ideas you can copy​

ScenarioDurationApproverRemote user
On-call break-glass1–2hAnother on-call / leadroot
Deploy to one app host30–60mService ownerdeploy
Vendor debug2–4hSecurity + ownerrestricted user
Homelab tinkering24hYouwhatever you use

FAQ​

Does JIT replace MFA?
No. MFA protects the console / identity; JIT limits when privileged relationships exist.

What if the approver is offline?
Define a backup approver rotation. A JIT system with one human bottleneck will be routed around.

Related​

  • Session recording is hard
  • Looking for early operators
  • Discord

item.title: Relationship-based access control (ReBAC) for SSH machines
item.description: How ReBAC maps users to SSH machines and remote users in a self-hosted PAM gateway, when to use OpenFGA, and how Orion Belt keeps grants practical.
item.content:encoded:
Most SSH fleets still authorize with a pile of authorized_keys files and a spreadsheet nobody updates. Relationship-based access control (ReBAC) is a clearer model for a self-hosted PAM gateway: users have relationships to machines (and to remote OS users), not a single “ops can root everywhere” bit.

This guide explains how ReBAC shows up in Orion Belt, how it differs from coarse RBAC, and when optional OpenFGA is worth introducing.

RBAC vs ReBAC in one paragraph​

RBAC says: Alice has role sre, and role sre may access production.
ReBAC says: Alice is can_access on machine web-01 as remote user deploy — a direct relationship in a graph.

RBAC scales via roles; ReBAC scales via relationships. SSH privileged access is naturally relational: person ↔ machine ↔ unix account ↔ time window.

How Orion Belt models it​

At a practical level:

  1. Users authenticate with SSH public keys (plus optional MFA on the console).
  2. Agents / machines enroll with the gateway.
  3. Grants attach a user to a machine with allowed remote users (root, ubuntu, deploy, …).
  4. JIT requests create time-bounded relationships instead of standing grants.

UI surfaces: Users, Permissions, Agents. CLI examples live in User management and the server setup docs.

orion-belt-server permission grant \
  --user alice --machine web-01 --type both --remote-users deploy

Remote users are still Linux users​

ReBAC does not invent OS accounts. If you grant alice → web-01 as root, the root account must exist on the agent host. Orion brokers the session; the agent host still enforces local account reality.

This is intentional. A PAM gateway should not pretend to replace /etc/passwd.

Optional OpenFGA​

Some organizations already run OpenFGA (or similar) as a central authorization service. Orion Belt can optionally delegate authorization checks there instead of only using built-in tables.

Use built-in ReBAC when:

  • You want the smallest self-hosted footprint
  • Your grant graph is “users ↔ machines”

Consider OpenFGA when:

  • Authorization is already centralized for other apps
  • You need richer tuple models shared outside SSH

Misconfiguring external authz is a footgun — fail closed, and test grants in a lab first.

Least privilege patterns that work​

  1. Default deny — no grant, no SSH through the gateway.
  2. Per-machine grants instead of “production” mega-roles when possible.
  3. Non-root remote users for day-to-day work; root via JIT.
  4. Separate auditor identities for session playback.
  5. Revoke on offboarding as a checklist item next to GitHub org removal.

FAQ​

Is this Zanzibar?
ReBAC as a family includes Zanzibar-style systems. Orion’s built-in path is the pragmatic SSH subset; OpenFGA is the escape hatch for fuller models.

Can groups map to many machines?
Use tags/metadata for organization, but remember: tags are not grants. Access still needs an explicit permission edge (or a higher-level automation that creates those edges).

Related​

  • JIT SSH access
  • Machine management
  • Orion Belt vs Teleport

item.title: Why SSH session recording is harder than access control
item.description: Building reliable SSH session recording for a self-hosted PAM gateway — PTY quirks, secret leakage, retention, encryption, and live watch. How Orion Belt approaches auditability.
item.content:encoded:
Granting “alice may SSH to web-01” is the easy half of privileged access. The hard half is answering, months later: what exactly happened in that shell? Access control is a graph. SSH session recording is a stream of messy terminal I/O, storage policy, and human behavior.

This article is for operators evaluating self-hosted PAM or an SSH bastion with audit requirements — SOC-minded startups, regulated teams, and homelabs that still want a paper trail.

Access control vs auditability​

ConcernTypical difficultyFailure mode
Authn (keys, MFA)MediumStolen key, weak MFA
Authz (who → which host)MediumOver-broad roles
RecordingHighGaps, unreadable casts, secret sprawl on disk
Live watchHighLatency, privacy, “who can watch whom”

Many DIY bastions stop after authz. Then an incident arrives and nobody can replay the session that mattered.

Why terminal recording is messy​

PTYs are not logs​

Interactive SSH is a pseudo-terminal: resize events, ANSI color, cursor motion, binary-ish output from top or vim. A naive “log stdout” approach produces files humans cannot replay. Orion Belt uses cast-style recordings suitable for console playback — the same family of ideas behind asciinema-like players.

Secrets show up in cleartext​

People paste API tokens into shells. Recordings then become secret stores. Treat recording storage like a vault:

  • Encrypt at rest when possible (recording.encryption_key in Orion)
  • Restrict who can open Sessions in the UI
  • Set retention deliberately
  • Train operators not to paste production secrets into recorded sessions (or use short-lived creds)

Completeness depends on the path​

If a user SSHs directly to the agent host, the gateway never sees the session. Recording is not magic — it only covers traffic that traverses the gateway. The operational rule has to be cultural and technical: gateway or it didn’t happen.

What “good” looks like in a PAM gateway​

For Orion Belt’s v1 SSH slice we care about:

  1. Record by default on the Docker / recommended path
  2. Playback in the web console (seek / pause)
  3. Live watch for active sessions when an admin needs eyes-on
  4. Compression + retention knobs for disk reality
  5. Optional encryption at rest

Web terminal sessions are first-class too (source=web) — not only CLI SSH.

Live watch vs recording​

RecordingLive watch
WhenAfter / during flushDuring active session
UseIncident review, compliancePairing, suspicious activity
RiskSecrets on diskPrivacy / over-surveillance

Both should be permissioned. Silent always-on screen sharing for every admin is how you lose trust inside the company.

Deep dive: Live session watch.

Implementation checklist for self-hosted teams​

Before you call your SSH bastion “auditable”:

  1. Force human SSH through the gateway (OpenSSH user+host@gw or osh)
  2. Enable recording; set retention days you can defend
  3. Turn on encryption at rest for anything beyond a lab
  4. Limit replay roles (auditor vs operator)
  5. Test a full loop: connect → type → disconnect → playback

Lab path: Try in 10 minutes ends on the Sessions page for a reason — that is the conversion moment.

FAQ​

Are recordings a substitute for shell history?
No. Histories are per-user, incomplete, and editable. Gateway recordings are a different control.

Can I disable recording?
Yes in config — not recommended if you claimed PAM-style audit to leadership.

Where are files stored?
Default /var/lib/orion-belt/recordings (or the Docker volume). Plan disk like you plan log volume.

Related​

  • Why reverse SSH agents
  • JIT access approvals
  • Sessions guide
  • GitHub

item.title: Announcing Orion Belt — open-source self-hosted SSH PAM / bastion
item.description: Orion Belt is a self-hosted SSH access gateway with PAM workflows — reverse agents, session recording, JIT approvals, MFA, and ReBAC. A lighter Teleport alternative for SSH-first teams.
item.content:encoded:
Orion Belt is an open-source, self-hosted SSH access gateway with PAM-style workflows. It sits in the gap between a raw jump host and a full zero-trust platform: reverse agents, session recording, live watch, JIT approvals, MFA/WebAuthn, ReBAC, and an optional SSH CA — without SaaS dependency.

This post is the product introduction for operators evaluating an open source PAM option focused on SSH, or a practical Teleport alternative when you do not need every protocol on day one.

Who this is for​

  • DevOps / SRE / Linux admins managing fleets over SSH
  • Homelab and self-hosted users who want audit trails without a vendor cloud
  • Small companies that need privileged access controls but not a CyberArk deployment
  • Teams tired of inbound :22 on every box “just for the bastion”

The problem we kept hitting​

Traditional SSH access fails in predictable ways:

  • Long-lived keys copied into chat
  • No native approval workflow for break-glass
  • Spotty or nonexistent session recording
  • Broad VPN access instead of per-machine grants
  • “Enterprise PAM” locked behind proprietary licensing and SaaS

Commercial products and large open platforms solve real problems — and often arrive with an ops surface larger than the SSH problem you actually have.

What Orion Belt is (and is not)​

It is​

A Linux-first SSH/SCP bastion gateway you run yourself:

CapabilityRole
Reverse agentsTargets dial out — no inbound hole required on the host for Orion
GatewayBrokers sessions, enforces ReBAC, records traffic
JIT accessRequest → approve → expire (UI / API / ChatOps)
MFATOTP + WebAuthn for the console; FIDO SSH keys supported
Clientsosh / ocp / oadmin, vanilla OpenSSH, or web terminal
PackagingDocker quickstart, deb/rpm/apk

It is not​

  • A multi-protocol mesh for Kubernetes, Windows RDP, and databases on day one (roadmap items exist; SSH is the v1 promise)
  • A hosted SaaS (by design)
  • A drop-in replacement for every Teleport feature

Positioning we use deliberately: Teleport-like SSH access control without opening inbound ports or adopting a large platform.

Architecture in one diagram​

CLI / OpenSSH / Web UI  →  Gateway (:2222 SSH, :8080 UI)
                              ↑ reverse SSH
                           Agent on target

Details: Architecture overview and Why reverse SSH agents.

License — said up front​

Orion Belt ships under Apache 2.0 + Commons Clause:

  • Free to use, modify, and self-host, including internal commercial use
  • You cannot sell Orion Belt (or a hosted service whose value derives substantially from it) as a product

We disclose this on community posts (HN, Lobsters, Reddit) because security software requires trust. Full text: LICENSE. More context: Commons Clause honesty.

Try it​

git clone https://github.com/orion-belt-dev/orion-belt.git
cd orion-belt
./scripts/docker-quickstart.sh

Walkthrough: Try Orion Belt in 10 minutes.

Links​

  • Website: orion-belt.dev
  • Source: github.com/orion-belt-dev/orion-belt
  • Chat: Discord
  • Compare: Orion Belt vs Teleport

item.title: Why reverse SSH agents beat opening inbound ports on every server
item.description: Learn how reverse SSH tunnels work for bastion and PAM gateways, the security tradeoffs versus inbound SSH, and why Orion Belt agents dial out instead of exposing port 22.
item.content:encoded:
If you run Linux servers at any scale, you already know the uncomfortable default: someone opens SSH from somewhere. A bastion host, a VPN, a “temporary” security-group rule that outlives the outage. Traditional SSH bastion designs still often assume the gateway (or the operator) can reach port 22 on the target.

Orion Belt takes a different default for its self-hosted PAM / SSH access gateway: agents on target machines dial out to the gateway over reverse SSH. Operators never need inbound SSH on the application hosts. This article explains why that model exists, how it compares to classic bastions, and which tradeoffs you should accept with eyes open.

The problem with inbound SSH (even “just from the bastion”)​

A classic pattern looks like this:

Operator → Bastion → (SSH :22) → Target

That path creates recurring pain:

  1. Firewall inventory grows forever — every VPC, VLAN, and cloud account needs rules so the bastion can reach targets.
  2. Targets stay in the attack surface — anything that accepts inbound SSH can be scanned, credential-stuffed, or hit by a compromised jump box.
  3. NAT and ephemeral environments fight you — laptops, CI runners, and locked-down cloud subnets often cannot be “reached”; they can only dial out.
  4. Audit is incomplete — if anyone still SSHs direct to the host, your bastion’s session recording never sees it.

A Teleport alternative or commercial PAM product will solve pieces of this with agents, workers, or IoT tunnels. The underlying idea is the same: move reachability so the target initiates connectivity.

What “agents dial out” means​

In Orion Belt:

Operator → Gateway (SSH :2222 / HTTPS UI)
                ↑
         reverse SSH
                ↑
              Agent on target
  1. You install a small agent on the machine you want to manage.
  2. The agent authenticates to the gateway and keeps a reverse tunnel open (outbound from the target’s perspective).
  3. When an operator connects to the gateway (CLI, OpenSSH user+machine@gateway, or web terminal), the gateway brokers the session through that tunnel.
  4. Policy (ReBAC), MFA on the console, JIT approvals, and session recording / live watch all sit on the gateway path.

The target still runs Linux users and SSH semantics for the remote account you land on (root, ubuntu, …). What changes is who is allowed to open a connection toward that host from the network.

Security tradeoffs (honest)​

Gains​

  • No inbound SSH required on targets for the Orion path — useful for hardened subnets and “deny all ingress” baselines.
  • One control plane for authentication, authorization, and recording.
  • Works behind NAT as long as the agent can reach the gateway on the SSH port (typically 2222).

Costs​

  • Offline agent = unreachable host until it reconnects. There is no magic wake-up if the process is dead.
  • The gateway becomes high-value infrastructure. Treat it like a domain controller for SSH: patch it, restrict who can reach :2222 / the UI, encrypt recordings, require MFA for admins.
  • You must trust and update the agent binary on every enrolled machine — same class of risk as any other node agent (Teleport, monitoring, backup).

If your environment cannot allow outbound connections from targets to a central gateway, reverse agents are the wrong tool. Pick a model that matches your network policy.

Reverse tunnels vs VPN vs classic bastion​

ApproachTypical exposureRecording / JITOps weight
Direct SSH to hostsInbound :22 everywhereDIYLow features
Classic bastionInbound to bastion + often bastion→host :22Rare / customLow–medium
Full-mesh VPNBroad L3 reachabilitySeparate toolsMedium
Reverse-agent gateway (Orion Belt)Inbound mainly to gateway; agents outboundBuilt-in pathMedium, SSH-focused

VPNs solve “how do packets route.” They do not automatically give you privileged access management: approvals, per-machine grants, or session playback. A self-hosted privileged access gateway sits above connectivity.

When reverse agents are the right default​

Choose this model if most of these are true:

  • Your privileged access problem is mostly SSH
  • You want a self-hosted SSH bastion without SaaS lock-in
  • Security wants no inbound SSH on app tiers
  • You need recording + JIT without buying a CyberArk-class appliance
  • You are okay operating a gateway (Postgres, backups, hardening)

Prefer a broader platform (for example Teleport) if you need first-class Kubernetes, database, Windows, and web app access in one product on day one. See our comparison: Orion Belt vs Teleport.

Try the model in about ten minutes​

The fastest way to feel the tradeoff is not a whitepaper:

git clone https://github.com/orion-belt-dev/orion-belt.git
cd orion-belt
./scripts/docker-quickstart.sh

Then follow Try Orion Belt in 10 minutes: register an agent, dial out, SSH through the gateway, open Sessions for playback.

FAQ​

Is reverse SSH the same as ssh -R?
Conceptually related: the remote side opens a channel back toward you. Orion Belt productizes that idea with enrollment, policy, and recording — not a one-off shell tunnel.

Do I still expose port 22 on the target?
Not for the Orion path. You may leave local SSH for break-glass; just know those sessions bypass gateway recording.

What about lateral movement from a compromised gateway?
A compromised gateway is bad in any bastion design. Mitigate with MFA, least privilege, network allowlists, recording encryption, and host hardening — see the main repo’s deployment hardening docs.

Next reading​

  • Announcing Orion Belt
  • Session recording is hard
  • Architecture overview
  • Discord · GitHub
